blurb: The big, bold garden tulip — goblet flowers in every saturated color that headline the mid-spring border, though here they often blaze brightest the first year and fade after.
water: Low; water at planting and in spring growth, then bake dry through summer dormancy
spread: Clumping; large hybrids tend to decline rather than spread and are often replanted
seasons: Showy goblet flowers mid-to-late spring (Apr–May); broad grey-green leaves spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground fall–winter
wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for bees when flowers are open; not a major wildlife plant
deer: Not deer-resistant — deer, rabbits, voles, and squirrels all browse or dig the buds and bulbs; protect new plantings
toxic: Toxic — all parts contain tulipalin (tulipalin A and B) and glycosides; ingestion causes vomiting, drooling, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, with the bulb most concentrated; handling bulbs can cause 'tulip fingers' (contact dermatitis)
origin: Garden hybrid; wild ancestors of Central Asia & Turkey
habitat: Steppe & rocky mountain slopes with hot, dry summers

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3a–8b — fully cold-hardy in Boulder's zone 5b–6a; the bulbs need a winter chill to bloom, which our winters supply automatically.[1] The catch on the Front Range is longevity, not cold: large hybrid (Darwin, Triumph, single late) tulips usually flower spectacularly the first spring, then decline markedly in the second year and may dwindle to leaves-only after that.[1][3]
care.planting: Plant bulbs in fall, mid-September through late October, once the soil has cooled but before it freezes, so roots establish over winter.[2] Set them pointed-end up at 2–3 times the bulb's height deep — roughly 6–8 in. for big hybrid bulbs (plant on the deeper end here, which helps perennialize and protects from frost heave and voles), about 4–6 in. apart.[2][3] Boulder's alkaline clay drains slowly; work coarse grit or sharp sand into the planting pocket and pick a spot that bakes dry in summer (a south- or west-facing slope, a hot foundation bed), since wet summer soil rots the bulbs. In containers, use a fast-draining mix in a deep pot, plant a bit shallower, and overwinter the pot in an unheated garage or cold frame so freeze-thaw doesn't heave or freeze the bulbs solid; container bulbs are far less cold-protected than in-ground ones.[2][3]
care.sun: Full sun — 6+ hours gives the sturdiest stems and best flowering; too much shade makes them stretch and bloom poorly. A spot that is sunny in spring is what matters, since the plant is dormant by summer.[1][3]
care.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil; tulips are intolerant of wet, poorly drained ground and rot in it.[1] Neutral to alkaline is ideal, so Boulder's pH suits them with no amendment; the priority is drainage — amend heavy clay planting holes with grit and raise the bed where water sits.[3]
care.water: Low. Water in well at fall planting to settle the soil and start roots, and again if fall stays dry. During spring growth and bloom, snowmelt and spring rain are usually enough; supplement only in a warm dry spell. Once the foliage yellows in early summer, stop watering entirely and let the soil bake dry through dormancy — summer moisture is the main killer of bulbs here.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space large bulbs about 4–6 in. apart (under 12 in. for a massed look); for a bold display, cluster bulbs of one variety together rather than dotting them singly.[1][3]
care.propagation: By offsets: large hybrids increase slowly and erratically here, but you can lift clumps in summer dormancy, separate any daughter bulblets from the mother bulb, and replant immediately at depth — small bulblets take a few years to reach flowering size. By seed: possible but very slow (5–7 years to bloom) and seedlings of a hybrid won't come true, so division/offsets is the practical route; most gardeners simply buy fresh bulbs to renew a fading planting.[1][3]
care.depth: Plant bulbs 2–3 times their height deep — about 6–8 in. for standard hybrid bulbs, going to the deeper end in light or sandy soil and to help them return.[2][3]
care.bloom: Mid-to-late spring on the Front Range, typically April into May depending on type and microclimate; each flower lasts one to two weeks and the whole show is brief.[1][3]
care.feeding: Optional. A low-nitrogen bulb fertilizer or bone meal worked in at fall planting, and a light feeding as shoots emerge in spring, supports the bulb; avoid high-nitrogen feeds, which push leaves over flowers. Lavish feeding will not overcome the inherent short life of big hybrids.[1]
care.maintenance: Let the foliage die back naturally after bloom — do not cut or braid green leaves, as they recharge the bulb for next year; remove them only once fully yellow.[2] Deadhead spent flowers to stop the plant wasting energy on seed. Because hybrids decline, plan to lift and discard exhausted bulbs and replant fresh every few years, or interplant with perennials/groundcovers that hide the fading leaves and carry the bed afterward.[2][3]
care.troubles: Bulb rot from wet, poorly drained or summer-irrigated soil is the chief failure.[1] Voles, squirrels, and chipmunks dig and eat bulbs (a wire-mesh cage or grit collar at planting helps), and deer and rabbits browse buds and flowers, so an unprotected planting may be eaten the night before it opens.[1] Tulip fire (Botrytis tulipae) can spot leaves and flowers in cool wet springs — destroy affected plants and don't replant tulips in the same spot for a few years.[3]
care.companions: Underplant or interplant with later-rising perennials and groundcovers — catmint, hardy geranium, daylily, or creeping phlox — that fill in as the tulip foliage fades; pair with daffodils and grape hyacinth for a layered spring-bulb display.[1]

edible.summary: Do not eat any part of a garden tulip — all parts are toxic, the bulb most of all, and handling bulbs can irritate skin.[1][4]
edible.caution: Every part of Tulipa gesneriana contains tulipalin A and B (a lactone), glycosides, and allergenic glycoproteins, with the toxin most concentrated in the bulb.[1][4] Ingestion causes vomiting, hypersalivation (drooling), depression, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, and stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting in people; large amounts can be more serious.[1][4] Tulip bulbs are sometimes mistaken for onions or shallots — never eat an unlabeled bulb. Handling the bulbs (especially peeling many of them) commonly causes an allergic contact dermatitis known as 'tulip fingers' — itching, redness, cracking, and blisters on the hands; wear gloves.[1] Keep bulbs and plants away from children and pets, and if ingestion is suspected call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435).[1][4]
